prompt: We built a data catalog last year. It has every table, every column, data types, primary keys, the works. We deployed an AI assistant to query it three months ago and it keeps querying the wrong tables. It'll join the Sales customers table to the Finance transactions table even though those customer_ids don't mean the same thing. What are we missing in our catalog?
expected: Skill activates and diagnoses that the catalog has only the technical layer (names, types, keys) while the business layer (owner/description distinguishing the Sales vs. Finance tables) and semantic layer (cross-domain equivalence mapping stating whether the two customer_ids are the same concept) are absent; without semantic grounding the AI fills the gap from its training prior and joins on matching column names, so both layers must be added before AI queries can be trusted.

prompt: Our data team documented everything in Confluence — table descriptions, column definitions, ownership. But our business analysts still can't find what they need and keep asking engineers for help. The documentation exists. Why isn't it working?
expected: Skill activates and identifies that documentation in a separate wiki is not equivalent to a business metadata layer co-located with the data asset; the three layers (technical, business, semantic) must live in the catalog attached to the tables/columns themselves so non-technical consumers can verify the right source without asking an engineer, and it should recommend migrating the Confluence content into the catalog as owned, in-place business metadata.

prompt: Two of our data domains — Marketing and Revenue Operations — both have an 'events' table. When we merge event streams for cross-domain analysis we get garbage results. Both tables have the same column names but the values mean different things. How should we have structured the metadata to prevent this?
expected: Skill activates and names this as context collapse caused by a missing semantic metadata layer: the technical column names survived the merge but the semantic encoding of what 'event' means in each Bounded Context and the cross-domain equivalence (or non-equivalence) mapping was never captured, so a join on matching names is a guess; the fix is to encode domain-scoped semantic metadata and explicit equivalence mappings before merging streams.

prompt: Sales and Finance both use the term 'revenue' but they calculate it differently — Sales includes pipeline, Finance counts only closed deals. We need to pick a canonical definition and make it the standard. How do we approach this?

Our AI agent has access to the customer table and keeps trying to update customer status flags directly, bypassing the approval workflow we have in our CRM. The data is correct — it's reading the right values. How do we constrain what the agent is allowed to do?

We're starting a new data governance initiative. Our CISO wants role-based access control on all tables. Our CDO wants 'better data documentation'. Our AI team wants to deploy an LLM assistant next quarter. What should we build first and how do we organize what we document?
expected: Skill activates and organizes the work around the three-layer stack: the CISO's RBAC is orthogonal and out of scope, the CDO's 'better documentation' and the AI team's LLM deployment are both answered by building technical, business, and semantic metadata as the foundation, and the semantic layer in particular must be complete before the AI assistant ships; it should recommend building the three-layer stack first (with CIA layered on later for the agent) rather than starting from RBAC.
